tokenColors.comment: Comments — quiet, italic, never competes with gold.

Pure C inside native{} / fields{} content — scoped as comment.block so it stays grey/uncolored under any theme, marking 'you have left Gata syntax.' Deliberately fully flat with NO sub-highlighting at all, not even for parens/brackets — unlike unsafe{}, this isn't real Gata code. The delimiting braces of native{} itself are NOT this scope — see keyword.control.risk.gata below.

Risk surface — unsafe, defer, throw/throws, panic, native/native type, and ONLY the outermost delimiting braces of native{}/unsafe{} (not nested if/while/etc. braces inside, and not parens/brackets — those follow #brackets like everywhere else). The one keyword family that's a warning, not a category: deep maroon, the single non-gold accent.
Structural/compiler-contract layer — kernel, user, process, thread, entry, fields, annotations. Muted violet so it sits quietly behind gold types without competing.
Declaration keywords — let, class, func, as, static, ... Cobalt blue, distinct from teal (functions), violet (structural), gold (types), and the branching-keyword green below.

Branching/looping/control-transfer — if, else, while, for, switch, case, try, catch, match, break, continue, ... 'return' lives here too (a control-transfer statement, like break/continue, not a declaration), as does 'default' (overwhelmingly a switch/match case label, pairing visually with 'case'). Sage green, deliberately a different hue from the declaration keywords above.
'self' isn't a real lexer keyword, but reads as one — same family as the storage/control keywords.
Method/function names — both declarations and call sites. Soft teal, distinct from both the blue keywords and the gold types.
true/false/null — pale champagne, part of the gold family since they're literal values of a type.
Primitive value types — bool, int, double, ... Gold, the language's signature color for data types.
Class/module reference types — multi-letter PascalCase. Brighter gold than primitives, still the same family.
Generic parameters — bare T/K/V/U-style single letters. Dim bronze, the muted end of the gold family.
Operators — barely distinguishable from plain variables/identifiers (a hair cooler/lighter), just enough to tell them apart on close reading without competing with the palette.
Semicolons — same neutral family as operators.

Parens and brackets — citrine/lime-yellow, deliberately a cooler, more green-leaning yellow than the warm golds used for types (#c9a227 primitives, #e0b34d classes) — close enough to still read as 'yellow' but far enough in hue that 'List[String]()' doesn't blur into one indistinguishable smear of gold. Applies everywhere (including inside unsafe{}/native{} — only the block's own outer { } go risk red, not these). Also why this extension disables VS Code's bracket-pair-colorization for .g files: so these don't get overridden by unrelated cycling colors.
A literal empty pair '()' — a no-arg call/ctor with nothing to draw attention to — renders grey instead of the yellow #brackets normally use.
Comma, dot, braces — one neutral scope, one color, everywhere outside a native block.
